extra.message: SQL injection in Student.create(). The user-controlled `name` parameter is interpolated directly into the SQL string via Python's `%` formatting operator (`'VALUES (\'%(name)s\')' % {'name': name}`) instead of being passed as a parameterized query argument. The resulting string is then executed with `await cur.execute(q)` with no parameter binding. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL by providing a malicious `name` value. The data flows from `sqli/views.py:57` via `data['name']` from user POST data.

Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via `{{ review.review_text }}`. Jinja2 autoescape is globally disabled (`autoescape=False` in `sqli/app.py:35`), so user-supplied `review_text` (submitted via `sqli/views.py:121`, stored in DB, then rendered here) is output without HTML escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by submitting a malicious review containing `<script>` tags. Additional unsafe template variable renderings include `{{ name }}` in `students.jinja2:16`, `{{ course.title }}` and `{{ course.description }}` in `courses.jinja2:17-18`, and `{{ course.title }}`/`{{ course.description }}` in `course.jinja2:14-15`.

Jinja2 autoescape is globally disabled via `autoescape=False` in the `setup_jinja()` call. This means ALL template variables are rendered without HTML escaping by default, enabling stored and reflected XSS attacks across the entire application wherever user-controlled data is rendered in templates. Only explicit `| e` filters (used inconsistently in a few templates like `student.jinja2`) provide escaping, leaving most template expressions vulnerable.

MD5 is used for password hashing in `User.check_password()`. MD5 is cryptographically broken and vulnerable to fast brute-force attacks (billions of hashes per second on consumer hardware). An attacker who obtains the `pwd_hash` from the database can quickly recover passwords. Additionally, MD5 is used without a salt, making rainbow table attacks trivial.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: RedisStorage is initialized with `httponly=False`, meaning the session cookie lacks the HttpOnly flag. This allows client-side JavaScript to access the session cookie via `document.cookie`. If any XSS vulnerability is present in the application (and multiple exist here), an attacker can trivially steal the session cookie and hijack authenticated user sessions.

CSRF middleware is explicitly commented out (`# csrf_middleware,`) in the application middleware list. While CSRF tokens are still generated and passed to templates via `csrf_processor`, they are never validated on POST requests. This leaves all mutation endpoints (`/students/`, `/courses/`, `/courses/*/review`, `/students/*/evaluate/*`, `/logout/`) vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery attacks. An attacker can trick authenticated users into performing unintended actions.

The 50x error template renders `{{ error.__dict__ }}`, which exposes the full internal state of HTTP exceptions to the user. This can leak sensitive information such as internal server paths, database error messages, connection strings, stack traces, and application internals. Combined with `debug=True` in `sqli/app.py:24`, this significantly increases information disclosure risk.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through an Error Message
